stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to a regional emergency department 6 hours after falling from a horse onto his head. He vomited twice in transit and his Glasgow Coma Scale was 14 on arrival. Over the next 90 minutes his GCS has dropped to 11 with reduced verbal response and localising to pain. His right pupil is 4 mm and sluggish, while the left is 3 mm and reactive. Blood pressure has risen from 110/65 to 140/55 and heart rate has fallen from 92 to 50. He has not yet been imaged. The retrieval team estimates 60 minutes to the nearest paediatric neurosurgical centre.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 50, RR 14 irregular, SpO2 97% on 6 L mask, BP 140/55, Temp 36.7
data_table.Examination: GCS 11 (E3 V3 M5); right pupil 4 mm sluggish, left 3 mm reactive; localising to pain; no focal limb weakness yet
data_table.Investigations: CT head not yet performed; FBC, UEC, coags pending

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Rapid sequence intubation, head-up 30 degrees, hypertonic saline 3 percent bolus, urgent CT head and neurosurgery transfer
options.rationale: Falling GCS with anisocoria, the Cushing reflex of hypertension and bradycardia, and respiratory irregularity indicates raised intracranial pressure with impending herniation; standard care is RSI with head-up positioning, hypertonic saline 3 percent bolus, and urgent imaging with definitive neurosurgical care. (Source: RCH CPG: Head Injury)

options.text: Perform a lumbar puncture to exclude a treatable cause of altered conscious state before transfer
options.rationale: Lumbar puncture in a patient with focal signs and likely raised intracranial pressure risks cerebral herniation and is absolutely contraindicated until imaging excludes mass effect. (Source: RCH CPG: Head Injury)

options.text: Empirical intravenous ceftriaxone and dexamethasone for likely bacterial meningitis after recent trauma
options.rationale: There is no fever, neck stiffness, rash, or history suggestive of meningitis; treating bacterial meningitis here delays the actual diagnosis of intracranial haemorrhage. (Source: RCH CPG: Head Injury)

options.text: Hyperventilate to a target PaCO2 of 25 mmHg as the primary measure to lower intracranial pressure
options.rationale: Aggressive hyperventilation to low PaCO2 risks ischaemic injury through vasoconstriction; PaCO2 is normalised at 35 to 40 mmHg with brief targeted reduction only for impending herniation as a temporising measure. (Source: RCH CPG: Head Injury)

options.text: Administer intravenous mannitol 1 g/kg via a peripheral line without securing the airway first
options.rationale: Mannitol may be used for raised intracranial pressure but is not the immediate single intervention; airway protection precedes osmotherapy and hypertonic saline is increasingly preferred in paediatric protocols. (Source: RCH CPG: Head Injury)

explanation.summary: Raised intracranial pressure with impending herniation in a child after head injury is identified by the Cushing reflex of hypertension and bradycardia, irregular respiration, falling GCS, anisocoria, and focal signs. Immediate care is airway protection through rapid sequence intubation, head-up 30 degrees, and a hypertonic saline 3 percent bolus, alongside expedited CT head and definitive neurosurgical care. Mannitol is an alternative osmotic agent but hypertonic saline is increasingly preferred in paediatric protocols. Hyperventilation is used only briefly as a temporising measure for herniation, and lumbar puncture is contraindicated.
key_points: Cushing reflex (hypertension + bradycardia + irregular breathing) signals impending herniation
Airway protection precedes osmotherapy
Hypertonic saline is increasingly preferred over mannitol in paediatric protocols
Lumbar puncture is contraindicated until imaging excludes mass effect
explanation.pearls: Head-up 30 degrees and 3 percent hypertonic saline 3 to 5 mL/kg is the immediate paediatric osmotherapy of choice; mannitol remains an alternative when sodium loading is contraindicated.

stem: A 35-year-old G4P3 at 36+4 weeks gestation is brought by ambulance after sudden severe abdominal pain following a motor vehicle collision. She has had minimal vaginal bleeding. Her abdomen is tense and tender with frequent uterine contractions on palpation. The CTG shows persistent foetal bradycardia at 80 bpm with reduced variability. Initial bloods show haemoglobin 88, platelets 110, fibrinogen 1.0 g/L, INR 1.6. Two large bore intravenous lines are placed and crystalloid started.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132, BP 86/52, SpO2 97% on 6 L mask, RR 26, Temp 36.4
data_table.Examination: Tense tender uterus with frequent contractions; minimal vaginal bleeding; CRT 4 s; pallor
data_table.Investigations: Hb 88, platelets 110, fibrinogen 1.0 g/L, INR 1.6; CTG: persistent foetal bradycardia 80 bpm

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Augment labour with an oxytocin infusion to expedite vaginal delivery in this multiparous woman with prior births
options.rationale: Foetal bradycardia and maternal haemodynamic instability mandate immediate delivery by caesarean; vaginal delivery is not appropriate when the foetus is decompensating and the mother is shocked. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Activate the massive transfusion protocol with early fibrinogen replacement and proceed to category 1 caesarean delivery
options.rationale: Concealed placental abruption with coagulopathy and foetal bradycardia is a category 1 emergency requiring activation of the massive transfusion protocol, early cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate to maintain fibrinogen above 2 g/L, and immediate caesarean delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Wait for cross-matched packed red cells before any intervention and treat with crystalloid alone in the meantime
options.rationale: Waiting for cross-matched blood while the foetus and mother decompensate is unsafe; O negative blood and the massive transfusion pack are used while cross-match completes. (Source: NBA Massive Transfusion Guidelines)

options.text: Administer fresh frozen plasma as the sole blood product to correct the coagulopathy before delivery
options.rationale: FFP alone does not replace fibrinogen rapidly; in obstetric haemorrhage with fibrinogen below 2 g/L cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate is essential, alongside packed cells and platelets. (Source: NBA Massive Transfusion Guidelines)

options.text: Give intravenous tranexamic acid as a single agent and continue conservative management for 30 minutes
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid is an adjunct to massive transfusion in major obstetric haemorrhage but is not a substitute for blood product resuscitation and delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

explanation.summary: Concealed placental abruption with foetal bradycardia and coagulopathy is an immediate threat to both mother and baby. A fibrinogen below 2 g/L in obstetric haemorrhage is a strong predictor of progression to massive haemorrhage and must be corrected early with cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate, not fresh frozen plasma alone. Initial resuscitation uses the massive transfusion protocol with packed red cells, fresh frozen plasma, platelets, and cryoprecipitate in a balanced ratio, with O negative blood while cross-match completes. Tranexamic acid is an adjunct. Delivery is by category 1 caesarean section. Postpartum surveillance for DIC and renal failure is essential.
key_points: Concealed abruption with foetal bradycardia is a category 1 caesarean indication
Fibrinogen below 2 g/L is a strong predictor of major haemorrhage
Cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate is required - FFP alone is insufficient
Tranexamic acid is an adjunct, not a substitute for blood products
explanation.pearls: Maintain fibrinogen above 2 g/L in obstetric haemorrhage; cryoprecipitate provides 30 to 50 mg/kg of fibrinogen and is faster than FFP.

subtopic_detail: Type B aortic dissection blood pressure target
stem: A 56-year-old man with poorly controlled hypertension presents to the emergency department with sudden severe tearing interscapular and chest pain 90 minutes ago. He is diaphoretic, distressed, and complains of a heavy feeling in both legs. His right arm blood pressure is 198/108 and his left arm is 162/92. ECG is in sinus rhythm with no ischaemic changes. A bedside contrast computed tomography aortogram confirms a type B aortic dissection beginning distal to the left subclavian artery with no end-organ malperfusion. He is otherwise haemodynamically stable.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96, BP right arm 198/108, left arm 162/92, SpO2 98% room air, RR 22, Temp 36.7
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic; equal radial pulses; bilateral leg heaviness without weakness; clear chest; no peritonitis
data_table.Investigations: CT aortogram: type B dissection distal to left subclavian; troponin negative; lactate 1.4; UEC normal

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: Commence intravenous glyceryl trinitrate as monotherapy targeting a systolic blood pressure below 120 mmHg
options.rationale: Nitrate monotherapy causes reflex tachycardia and increases aortic shear stress; rate control with a beta blocker must precede or accompany vasodilator therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Administer intravenous nicardipine as monotherapy and titrate to systolic blood pressure 100 to 120 mmHg
options.rationale: Calcium channel blockers without rate control likewise trigger reflex tachycardia and worsen shear stress; rate control through beta blockade is the priority. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Administer thrombolysis with intravenous tenecteplase for presumed inferior myocardial infarction
options.rationale: Thrombolysis in aortic dissection is catastrophic; the ECG and imaging exclude infarction and confirm dissection. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Commence intravenous esmolol or labetalol to a heart rate below 60 then add nitroprusside if needed
options.rationale: In acute aortic dissection initial therapy is intravenous beta blockade to a heart rate below 60 to reduce aortic shear stress, with a vasodilator such as sodium nitroprusside added if systolic pressure remains above 120 mmHg; the goal is to reduce both pulse pressure and absolute pressure. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Refer for emergency open surgical repair as definitive management of all uncomplicated type B dissections
options.rationale: Uncomplicated type B dissection without malperfusion or rupture is initially managed medically; surgery or thoracic endovascular aortic repair is reserved for complicated disease. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

explanation.summary: Acute aortic dissection management focuses on reducing aortic wall shear stress, which is proportional to the rate of rise of pressure and to heart rate. First-line intravenous beta blockade with esmolol or labetalol is titrated to a heart rate below 60 bpm before adding a vasodilator such as sodium nitroprusside to achieve a systolic pressure of 100 to 120 mmHg. Vasodilator monotherapy is contraindicated because reflex tachycardia accelerates dissection propagation. Type A dissection involves the ascending aorta and requires emergency surgery. Uncomplicated type B dissection beginning distal to the left subclavian is managed medically; complicated type B with malperfusion or rupture requires TEVAR or surgery.
key_points: Reduce heart rate to below 60 before adding a vasodilator
Target systolic blood pressure 100 to 120 mmHg in acute dissection
Vasodilator monotherapy increases shear stress and is contraindicated
Uncomplicated type B dissection is initially medical, not surgical
explanation.pearls: Esmolol's short half-life allows rapid titration in dissection; achieve heart rate below 60 before reaching for nitroprusside or nicardipine.

sources.label: Heart Foundation Australia: Acute Aortic Syndromes

stem: A 24-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by ambulance 5 hours after taking approximately 50 of her mother's amitriptyline 25 mg tablets in a deliberate self-harm episode. She has had a brief seizure during transit. On arrival she is drowsy with a GCS of 11, dry mucous membranes, mydriasis, and a tachycardia of 130. Her blood pressure is 90/58. An ECG shows sinus tachycardia with a QRS duration of 140 ms and a terminal R wave in aVR of 5 mm. Capillary glucose is 5.2 and pH from a venous gas is 7.30 with a lactate of 4.0.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 130, RR 22, SpO2 98% on 6 L mask, BP 90/58, Temp 36.8
data_table.Examination: GCS 11; dry mucous membranes; mydriasis; warm dry skin; bowel sounds absent
data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus tachycardia QRS 140 ms, R wave in aVR 5 mm; venous pH 7.30, lactate 4.0; glucose 5.2

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Administer activated charcoal 50 g via nasogastric tube as the immediate priority for decontamination
options.rationale: Activated charcoal is reserved for presentations within 1 to 2 hours with a protected airway; at 5 hours and a depressed conscious level it adds aspiration risk without meaningful absorption benefit. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Administer intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg to reverse the depressed conscious level
options.rationale: Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines, not TCAs, and in mixed overdose risks precipitating seizures; it is contraindicated in TCA toxicity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg as a bolus targeting a serum pH of 7.45 to 7.55
options.rationale: QRS prolongation above 100 ms in TCA overdose indicates cardiotoxicity and warrants sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg to a target pH of 7.45 to 7.55, which displaces TCA from sodium channels and narrows the QRS; bolus is repeated as needed and infusion may follow. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Administer intramuscular physostigmine as an anticholinergic reversal agent for the toxidrome
options.rationale: Physostigmine in TCA overdose precipitates asystole and seizures and is contraindicated; the cardiotoxicity is sodium channel blockade, not pure anticholinergic excess. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Administer intravenous calcium gluconate 10 percent 10 mL to stabilise the cardiac membrane
options.rationale: Calcium is used in hyperkalaemia and in calcium channel blocker overdose; it has no role in TCA cardiotoxicity, where sodium loading with bicarbonate is the specific intervention. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

explanation.summary: TCA overdose causes sodium channel blockade with QRS prolongation, anticholinergic toxidrome, seizures, hypotension, and refractory ventricular arrhythmias. ECG features predictive of toxicity include QRS above 100 ms and a terminal R wave in aVR above 3 mm. Sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg is the specific antidote, working through sodium loading and alkalinisation to narrow the QRS and stabilise the membrane; it is repeated until QRS narrows and pH reaches 7.45 to 7.55. Adjuncts include benzodiazepines for seizures, intubation, and noradrenaline for refractory hypotension. Lipid emulsion is considered for refractory cardiotoxicity. Flumazenil and physostigmine are contraindicated.
key_points: QRS above 100 ms or R wave in aVR above 3 mm predicts TCA cardiotoxicity
Sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg bolus is the specific antidote
Target pH is 7.45 to 7.55 with QRS narrowing as the clinical endpoint
Flumazenil and physostigmine are contraindicated in TCA overdose
explanation.pearls: The R wave in aVR is the most sensitive ECG predictor of TCA toxicity; above 3 mm warrants pre-emptive bicarbonate even if QRS is still under 100 ms.

stem: A 22-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department 2 hours after his mother found him with an open bottle of her ferrous sulfate 325 mg tablets. She estimates 10 tablets may be missing, giving an exposure of approximately 65 mg/kg of elemental iron. He has vomited twice and passed one loose grey-coloured stool. He is alert and crying. He weighs 12 kg. An abdominal radiograph shows several radio-opaque tablets in the upper gastrointestinal tract.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 142, RR 32, SpO2 99% room air, BP 100/65, Temp 36.8
data_table.Examination: Alert and crying; mildly distressed; soft tender epigastrium; CRT 2 s; no shock
data_table.Investigations: Abdominal X-ray: tablets visible in stomach; serum iron and venous gas sent at 4 hours post ingestion; UEC, FBC, LFTs pending

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Administer activated charcoal 1 g/kg orally as soon as airway is protected for gastrointestinal decontamination
options.rationale: Activated charcoal does not bind iron and adds no benefit while increasing aspiration risk; it has no role in iron poisoning. (Source: NSW Poisons Information Centre: Iron)

options.text: Administer empirical intravenous deferoxamine 15 mg/kg/hour before serum iron results are available
options.rationale: Deferoxamine is reserved for serum iron above 90 micromol/L taken 4 to 6 hours post ingestion, shock, altered conscious state, or persistent acidosis; empirical treatment is unnecessary in a well child with imaging visible tablets. (Source: NSW Poisons Information Centre: Iron)

options.text: Perform orogastric lavage with warm saline to clear the residual iron tablets from the stomach immediately
options.rationale: Gastric lavage is not routinely used in iron overdose and risks aspiration and oesophageal injury; whole-bowel irrigation is the preferred decontamination when tablets remain visible. (Source: NSW Poisons Information Centre: Iron)

options.text: Discharge home with safety advice because vomiting is a sign of likely self-decontamination and recovery
options.rationale: Iron ingestion above 40 mg/kg requires observation for 6 hours and a serum iron at 4 to 6 hours because the latent phase between gastrointestinal symptoms and shock can lull clinicians into a false reassurance. (Source: NSW Poisons Information Centre: Iron)

options.text: Commence polyethylene glycol whole-bowel irrigation and serum iron at 4 hours, with deferoxamine if iron is above 90 micromol/L
options.rationale: With residual radio-opaque tablets and ingestion above 40 mg/kg of elemental iron, polyethylene glycol whole-bowel irrigation clears the gut, while serum iron at 4 to 6 hours guides deferoxamine, which is started for iron above 90 micromol/L, shock, altered conscious state, or persistent acidosis. (Source: NSW Poisons Information Centre: Iron)

explanation.summary: Iron poisoning has five recognised phases: gastrointestinal in the first 6 hours, a latent phase from 6 to 24 hours, shock and metabolic acidosis from 12 to 48 hours, hepatic injury, and late gastrointestinal scarring. Activated charcoal does not bind iron and is not used. Whole-bowel irrigation with polyethylene glycol is the preferred decontamination when radio-opaque tablets remain in the gut. Serum iron at 4 to 6 hours guides deferoxamine therapy, with treatment initiated for serum iron above 90 micromol/L, shock, altered conscious state, or persistent acidosis. Even asymptomatic children with iron ingestion above 40 mg/kg require observation through the latent phase.
key_points: Activated charcoal does not bind iron
Whole-bowel irrigation is the decontamination of choice when tablets remain in the gut
Serum iron at 4 to 6 hours guides deferoxamine therapy
The latent phase from 6 to 24 hours masks deterioration; observe through it
explanation.pearls: A deferoxamine threshold of serum iron 90 micromol/L plus the trigger of shock or persistent acidosis covers nearly all cases; clinical state always trumps a borderline number.

stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 at 17+3 weeks gestation presents to the emergency department with 6 hours of sudden severe right iliac fossa pain and three episodes of vomiting. The pain woke her from sleep and has not improved with paracetamol. Her urinary beta-human chorionic gonadotrophin confirms the pregnancy and the pregnancy is intrauterine and viable on point of care ultrasound. A formal transvaginal ultrasound shows a right ovarian mass of 8 cm with absent doppler flow, and the left ovary is normal. White cell count is 13.5 with normal C-reactive protein. Urinalysis is bland.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110, BP 116/72, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 37.2
data_table.Examination: Tender right iliac fossa with guarding; uterus consistent with dates; no peritonitis elsewhere
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG positive; intrauterine viable pregnancy; right ovarian mass 8 cm with no doppler flow; left ovary normal; WCC 13.5; CRP 6

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit for observation with analgesia and serial ultrasound, planning intervention only if pain persists beyond 24 hours
options.rationale: Observation in suspected ovarian torsion risks irreversible ovarian necrosis within hours; the diagnosis mandates urgent surgical intervention. (Source: RANZCOG: Adnexal Mass in Pregnancy)

options.text: Proceed to laparoscopic detorsion with ovarian preservation and cystectomy as indicated by intraoperative findings
options.rationale: Sudden severe pelvic pain with an enlarged ovary and absent doppler flow is ovarian torsion; urgent laparoscopic detorsion with ovarian conservation is the standard of care, even when the ovary appears dusky, as recovery of function is common. (Source: RANZCOG: Adnexal Mass in Pregnancy)

options.text: Plan elective oophorectomy as definitive management because preservation of an ischaemic ovary risks thromboembolic complications
options.rationale: Routine oophorectomy is not standard; current evidence supports detorsion and conservation even in apparently necrotic ovaries because viable tissue often recovers, and thromboembolic risk does not warrant pre-emptive removal. (Source: RANZCOG: Adnexal Mass in Pregnancy)

options.text: Perform transvaginal cyst aspiration under ultrasound guidance to decompress the mass and relieve symptoms
options.rationale: Aspiration does not address torsion or restore blood flow and risks spillage of malignant cells if the mass is neoplastic; surgical detorsion is required. (Source: RANZCOG: Adnexal Mass in Pregnancy)

options.text: Commence nifedipine for tocolysis and admit for observation with daily foetal heart rate monitoring
options.rationale: Tocolysis does not treat ovarian torsion and observation delays definitive surgery; surgical intervention is safe at this gestation. (Source: RANZCOG: Adnexal Mass in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Ovarian torsion in pregnancy is a surgical emergency that presents with sudden severe iliac fossa pain, vomiting, and an enlarged ovary with reduced or absent doppler flow on ultrasound. Doppler flow can occasionally remain present due to dual ovarian blood supply, so clinical suspicion drives the diagnosis. Standard of care is urgent laparoscopic detorsion with ovarian conservation; even ovaries that appear dusky or necrotic can recover function. Oophorectomy is reserved for clearly necrotic or malignant masses. Surgery is safe in the second trimester and is preferred to expectant management because of the risk of ovarian loss.
key_points: Sudden severe iliac fossa pain plus enlarged ovary plus absent doppler equals torsion
Laparoscopic detorsion with ovarian conservation is first-line
Even dusky ovaries often recover; routine oophorectomy is not warranted
Surgery is safe in the second trimester and is preferred to observation
explanation.pearls: Doppler flow being present does not exclude torsion - dual ovarian supply from uterine and ovarian arteries means flow can persist; clinical features and ovarian size drive the call to theatre.

stem: A 45-year-old previously well woman presents to the emergency department with 36 hours of right flank pain, fever, rigors, dysuria, and three episodes of vomiting. She has no history of urinary tract infection and is not pregnant. Examination reveals right costovertebral angle tenderness and a soft non-peritonitic abdomen. Urinalysis shows leucocytes 3+, nitrites positive, and blood 1+. Bloods show a white cell count of 18.5, lactate of 2.4, and creatinine of 95. A urine culture has been sent. She is unable to tolerate oral fluids.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 118, BP 96/58, SpO2 98% room air, Temp 39.2, RR 22
data_table.Examination: Right costovertebral angle tenderness; soft non-peritonitic abdomen; warm peripheries
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis: leucocytes 3+, nitrites positive; WCC 18.5; lactate 2.4; creatinine 95; urine culture sent; beta-hCG negative

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Intravenous amoxicillin 2 g 6-hourly plus gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily after senior review of risks
options.rationale: Australian guidance for pyelonephritis requiring admission is IV gentamicin plus IV amoxicillin or ampicillin, with gentamicin dose adjusted for renal function and serum levels monitored; this covers the common Enterobacteriaceae and enterococci. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral trimethoprim 300 mg daily for 14 days with outpatient follow-up of urine culture
options.rationale: Oral monotherapy is appropriate for uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection only; this patient meets pyelonephritis criteria with systemic features and cannot tolerate oral fluids. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin 1.5 g loading dose targeting MRSA cover for community-acquired sepsis
options.rationale: Vancomycin is not part of empirical pyelonephritis cover in the absence of MRSA risk factors; Gram-negative Enterobacteriaceae are the dominant pathogens. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous metronidazole 500 mg 8-hourly with intravenous fluconazole pending culture results
options.rationale: Anaerobic and fungal cover is not required for uncomplicated pyelonephritis; the pathogens are Gram-negative aerobes and enterococci. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral nitrofurantoin 100 mg 6-hourly for 7 days with intravenous fluid resuscitation for vomiting
options.rationale: Nitrofurantoin achieves adequate concentration in the bladder but not in renal parenchyma and is contraindicated in pyelonephritis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Pyelonephritis requiring admission is treated with intravenous antibiotics targeting Enterobacteriaceae and enterococci. Australian guidance recommends intravenous amoxicillin or ampicillin plus gentamicin as first-line empirical therapy, with gentamicin dose adjusted for renal function and levels monitored if therapy continues beyond 48 hours. Switch to oral therapy is guided by clinical improvement, ability to tolerate oral intake, and urine culture sensitivities, with a total duration of 10 to 14 days. Nitrofurantoin and oral trimethoprim do not achieve renal parenchymal concentrations and are reserved for uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection. Imaging is considered for treatment failure, recurrent infection, or suspected obstruction.
key_points: Australian first-line empirical pyelonephritis cover is IV gentamicin plus amoxicillin or ampicillin
Nitrofurantoin is reserved for cystitis as renal parenchymal levels are inadequate
Oral switch is guided by clinical improvement and culture sensitivities
Total course is 10 to 14 days
explanation.pearls: Always send a urine culture before starting empirical pyelonephritis cover; the culture refines the second 7 to 10 days of therapy and detects resistance early.

stem: A 3-year-old boy is brought to a paediatric clinic by his mother who has noticed a firm abdominal mass while bathing him over the last 2 weeks. She also reports irritability, weight loss, and easy bruising of the eyelids that she initially attributed to a fall. He has been febrile intermittently. On examination he is pale and miserable with a firm fixed left upper abdominal mass that crosses the midline, periorbital ecchymoses without trauma, and blanching subcutaneous nodules. His blood pressure is 100/65. Urinary catecholamines are elevated. An MIBG scan shows tracer avidity in the primary mass and in skull lesions.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132, BP 100/65, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 37.6, weight 14 kg (50th centile previously, now 25th)
data_table.Examination: Pale and miserable; firm fixed left upper abdominal mass crossing midline; periorbital ecchymoses; blanching subcutaneous nodules
data_table.Investigations: Urinary catecholamines elevated; MIBG: tracer avidity in primary mass and skull lesions; FBC: pancytopenia

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Plan urgent nephrectomy as definitive management for the abdominal mass and submit specimen for histology
options.rationale: The mass crosses the midline and shows MIBG-avid metastases with elevated catecholamines, consistent with neuroblastoma not Wilms tumour; primary nephrectomy is the wrong operation. (Source: eviQ Paediatric Neuroblastoma)

options.text: Commence oral corticosteroids as a trial for likely autoimmune cytopenia with consequent ecchymoses
options.rationale: The combination of abdominal mass, MIBG-positive skull deposits, and elevated catecholamines indicates metastatic neuroblastoma; corticosteroid trial for autoimmune cytopenia is inappropriate and delays oncology care. (Source: eviQ Paediatric Neuroblastoma)

options.text: Reassure the family that periorbital ecchymoses are likely traumatic and review again in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Bilateral periorbital ecchymoses in the absence of clear trauma plus a fixed abdominal mass are red flags for metastatic neuroblastoma; reassurance and delay are unsafe. (Source: eviQ Paediatric Neuroblastoma)

options.text: Refer urgently to paediatric oncology for tissue diagnosis, full staging and risk-adapted multidisciplinary management
options.rationale: The presentation of a midline-crossing abdominal mass with bilateral periorbital ecchymoses (raccoon eyes), MIBG-avid skull metastases, and elevated catecholamines is metastatic neuroblastoma; urgent oncology referral for tissue diagnosis, staging, and risk-adapted treatment is the standard of care. (Source: eviQ Paediatric Neuroblastoma)

options.text: Plan a renal biopsy under ultrasound guidance to confirm Wilms tumour before any further intervention
options.rationale: Wilms tumour does not typically cross the midline, lacks MIBG avidity, and does not produce catecholamines; renal biopsy targets the wrong diagnosis. (Source: eviQ Paediatric Neuroblastoma)

explanation.summary: Neuroblastoma is the commonest extracranial solid tumour of childhood and arises from sympathetic neural crest tissue. It typically presents in children under 5 years with an abdominal mass that crosses the midline, distinguishing it from Wilms tumour which usually remains confined to one side. Periorbital ecchymoses or so called raccoon eyes reflect orbital metastases, while subcutaneous blueberry muffin nodules and bone marrow involvement complete the syndrome. Urinary catecholamines and MIBG scintigraphy are diagnostic. Treatment depends on stage and risk stratification, including INSS stage, MYCN amplification, and age. Low-risk disease may regress spontaneously, while high-risk disease requires multimodal therapy including surgery, chemotherapy, autologous stem cell transplant, and immunotherapy with anti-GD2.
key_points: Neuroblastoma crosses midline; Wilms typically does not
Bilateral periorbital ecchymoses without trauma signal orbital metastasis
Urinary catecholamines plus MIBG scintigraphy clinch the diagnosis
Risk-adapted management is determined by stage, MYCN, and age
explanation.pearls: Bilateral racoon eyes without trauma in a toddler plus an abdominal mass crossing midline equals neuroblastoma until proven otherwise; do not biopsy the kidney.

stem: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman is referred to a gynaecology clinic with 12 months of progressive vulval pruritus and soreness that worsens at night. She has had no postcoital bleeding. On examination there are well-demarcated white wrinkled plaques on the labia minora and perineum, with loss of the labia minora architecture and narrowing of the introitus producing a figure-of-eight appearance around the vulva and anus. A vulval punch biopsy taken at this visit confirms lichen sclerosus with no atypia. She has used emollient creams alone with no benefit.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78, BP 132/82, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.7
data_table.Examination: White wrinkled vulval plaques in a figure-of-eight pattern; loss of labia minora; introital narrowing; no ulceration
data_table.Investigations: Vulval punch biopsy: lichen sclerosus, no atypia; cervical screening up to date and negative

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Topical hydrocortisone 1 percent applied twice daily for 2 weeks then as required for flares
options.rationale: Mild topical steroids are insufficient to control vulval lichen sclerosus; under-treatment increases scarring and the lifetime risk of vulval squamous cell carcinoma. (Source: RANZCOG: Vulval Disorders)

options.text: Vaginal oestrogen pessaries inserted nightly for 4 weeks then twice weekly as maintenance therapy
options.rationale: Topical oestrogen treats vulvovaginal atrophy, not lichen sclerosus; while it may be a comfort measure, it does not treat the underlying lichenoid inflammation. (Source: RANZCOG: Vulval Disorders)

options.text: Topical clobetasol propionate 0.05 percent ointment with a taper over 12 weeks then maintenance plus annual review
options.rationale: Australian guidance treats vulval lichen sclerosus with potent topical steroid such as clobetasol propionate 0.05 percent, initially nightly for 4 weeks, alternate nights for 4 weeks, then twice weekly maintenance, with annual review for the 4 to 5 percent lifetime risk of vulval squamous cell carcinoma. (Source: RANZCOG: Vulval Disorders)

options.text: Topical antifungal clotrimazole cream applied twice daily for 4 weeks with a planned skin biopsy if no improvement
options.rationale: Lichen sclerosus is not infective and antifungal therapy is ineffective; biopsy has already confirmed the diagnosis. (Source: RANZCOG: Vulval Disorders)

options.text: Refer for total vulvectomy as definitive treatment to address scarring and prevent malignant transformation
options.rationale: Vulvectomy is not indicated for uncomplicated lichen sclerosus; surgery is reserved for biopsy-confirmed dysplasia or carcinoma, or for symptomatic adhesions. (Source: RANZCOG: Vulval Disorders)

explanation.summary: Vulval lichen sclerosus is a chronic inflammatory dermatosis that presents with pruritus, soreness, white wrinkled plaques, architectural loss, and the figure-of-eight perineal-perianal pattern. It carries a 4 to 5 percent lifetime risk of vulval squamous cell carcinoma and warrants annual clinical review. First-line treatment is a potent topical corticosteroid, classically clobetasol propionate 0.05 percent ointment, applied nightly for 4 weeks, alternate nights for 4 weeks, and then twice weekly indefinitely as maintenance. Emollients and avoidance of irritants support the regimen. Surgery is reserved for adhesions or biopsy-confirmed neoplasia. Symptoms refractory to potent steroid warrant repeat biopsy to exclude differentiated vulval intraepithelial neoplasia.
key_points: Lichen sclerosus is treated with potent topical steroid as first-line, not mild steroid or antifungal
Annual review captures the 4 to 5 percent lifetime risk of vulval SCC
Maintenance therapy twice weekly is required to prevent flares and scarring
Repeat biopsy if refractory to potent steroid to exclude dVIN
explanation.pearls: Clobetasol 0.05 percent ointment nightly for 4 weeks, alternate nights for 4 weeks, then twice weekly maintenance is the AU standard; annual review is non-negotiable.

sources.label: Australasian College of Dermatologists: Lichen Sclerosus

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with 2 weeks of progressive painful loss of central vision in the right eye and a relative afferent pupillary defect. Six months ago she had transient left-sided weakness and numbness that resolved spontaneously over 3 weeks without medical assessment. MRI brain today shows multiple T2 hyperintense lesions in the periventricular and juxtacortical white matter, one of which enhances with gadolinium, plus a non-enhancing lesion in the cervical spinal cord. Cerebrospinal fluid shows two oligoclonal bands not present in serum. She is otherwise well and has no family history of demyelinating disease.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 72, BP 118/72, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.6
data_table.Examination: Right relative afferent pupillary defect; reduced colour vision; otherwise normal neurological examination
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain: multiple T2 lesions, one gadolinium enhancing; MRI spine: non-enhancing cervical lesion; CSF: 2 unique oligoclonal bands

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Defer all immunomodulatory therapy until a second clinical relapse confirms relapsing-remitting disease
options.rationale: Current evidence and McDonald 2017 criteria support diagnosis of multiple sclerosis after a single clinical event when imaging and CSF satisfy dissemination in space and time; deferring disease-modifying therapy loses prevention years. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Commence intravenous immunoglobulin as a maintenance disease-modifying therapy with quarterly infusions
options.rationale: Intravenous immunoglobulin is not a first-line disease-modifying therapy in multiple sclerosis and is reserved for selected indications such as pregnancy planning; established DMT classes are preferred. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Prescribe oral prednisolone 50 mg daily for 6 months as long-term immunosuppression for relapse prevention
options.rationale: Long-term oral corticosteroids do not modify multiple sclerosis disease course and cause cumulative adverse effects; pulse intravenous methylprednisolone is used for acute relapses only. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Plan whole brain irradiation as definitive management of inflammatory demyelinating disease activity
options.rationale: Whole brain irradiation has no role in multiple sclerosis and would cause profound neurotoxicity; immunomodulatory disease-modifying therapy is the standard of care. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Treat acute optic neuritis with pulse methylprednisolone and initiate disease-modifying therapy promptly
options.rationale: McDonald 2017 allows multiple sclerosis diagnosis when dissemination in space (multiple typical regions) and time (gadolinium-enhancing plus non-enhancing lesions, or positive CSF oligoclonal bands) are met; treatment is pulse intravenous methylprednisolone for the acute optic neuritis and prompt initiation of disease-modifying therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

explanation.summary: McDonald 2017 criteria allow diagnosis of relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis after a single clinical event when dissemination in space and time are demonstrated by imaging or by positive cerebrospinal fluid oligoclonal bands. Acute optic neuritis is treated with pulse intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days, which speeds recovery but does not change long-term outcome. Disease-modifying therapy is initiated as soon as diagnosis is confirmed because early therapy reduces long-term disability; high-efficacy options including ocrelizumab and natalizumab are increasingly used first-line in patients with active disease. Pregnancy planning, comorbidities, and JC virus serology guide drug selection.
key_points: McDonald 2017 allows diagnosis from a single attack when DIS and DIT are met
Positive CSF oligoclonal bands can substitute for DIT under McDonald 2017
Pulse methylprednisolone treats acute relapses; DMT modifies disease course
Early initiation of high-efficacy DMT improves long-term outcomes
explanation.pearls: Oligoclonal bands in CSF that are not present in serum substitute for dissemination in time under McDonald 2017, allowing diagnosis from a single relapse.
